source_platforms.name: MSU/ORNL Power System Attack Datasets
source_platforms.writeup: Pan et al., IEEE Trans. Smart Grid, 2015

source_platforms.name: Liu, Ning & Reiter 2011
source_platforms.writeup: Mathematical proof that a=Hc attack bypasses chi-squared detection

ground_truth_references: Z-score catches Type 1 (large Q deviations) but misses subtle Type 2/3 attacks
Isolation Forest captures multivariate correlations for coordinated attacks
FDI implementation: J(x_a) ≈ J(x) when attack vector lies in column space of H
de_biasing_applied: Anomaly detection: evaluation by precision/recall/F1 against labeled ground truth
FDI simulation: success verified by chi-squared test bypass, not memorized attack vectors

tasks.prompt: Write an anomaly detection script for inverter telemetry data. Load the provided CSV containing 1000 inverter telemetry samples (with 50 injected attack samples). Implement both a z-score-based detector and an Isolation Forest detector, flag anomalous samples from each method, and evaluate detection performance (precision, recall, F1) against the ground truth labels. Report which method performs better and analyze the types of attack samples each method catches or misses.

type_1_reactive_power_injection.description: Q values inconsistent with volt-var curve given the measured voltage — reactive power commands injected via compromised Modbus write registers
type_1_reactive_power_injection.magnitude: Q deviations of 40-110 kVAR from expected volt-var output

type_2_voltage_measurement_manipulation.description: V readings offset by 0.02-0.05 pu from true values while P, Q, f remain normal — sensor data tampering
type_2_voltage_measurement_manipulation.magnitude: Subtle enough that individual readings may be within normal operating range

type_3_coordinated_PQ_attack.description: Simultaneous manipulation of P and Q to maintain apparent power factor while changing real power output — sophisticated command injection
type_3_coordinated_PQ_attack.magnitude: P shifted by 30-80 kW, Q adjusted to maintain original power factor angle

z_score_detector.description: Compute z-scores for each feature (P, Q, V, f) independently. Flag samples where any feature z-score exceeds the threshold.
z_score_detector.note: Use robust z-scores with median and MAD (Median Absolute Deviation) to reduce sensitivity to outliers in training statistics

isolation_forest_detector.description: Train an Isolation Forest on all four features (P, Q, V, f).

success_criteria.must_include_analysis: Explain which attack types are harder to detect and why

instructions: Perform exploratory data analysis: compute summary statistics, plot distributions of each feature, visualize normal vs attack samples.
Implement the robust z-score detector: compute median and MAD for each feature from the full dataset, flag samples exceeding threshold on any feature.
Implement the Isolation Forest detector with specified parameters.
For each detector, compute precision, recall, F1, and confusion matrix against the label column.
Break down detection performance by attack type (use the magnitude/pattern characteristics to classify detected attacks).
Generate ROC curves for both methods (vary z-score threshold / Isolation Forest decision function threshold).
Produce a final comparison table and analysis of strengths/weaknesses of each method.

tasks.ground_truth: Expected results: The z-score detector should perform well on Type 1 attacks (reactive power injection — large Q deviations are easily caught by univariate z-scores, expected detection rate >90%) but poorly on Type 2 (subtle voltage offsets within normal range, expected detection rate 30-50%) and Type 3 (coordinated P-Q attacks that maintain power factor, making individual features appear less anomalous, expected detection rate 40-60%). Expected z-score overall: precision ~0.60-0.75, recall ~0.55-0.70, F1 ~0.57-0.72. The Isolation Forest should perform better overall because it captures multivariate correlations — it should detect Type 3 attacks (coordinated P-Q shifts create unusual feature combinations even if individual features are in range, expected detection rate 60-80%) and Type 2 (subtle voltage manipulation detectable through V-P and V-Q correlations, expected detection rate 50-70%). Type 1 attacks should still be well-detected (>85%). Expected Isolation Forest overall: precision ~0.65-0.80, recall ~0.65-0.80, F1 ~0.65-0.80. Key analysis points: (1) Univariate methods fail on coordinated attacks that maintain inter-variable relationships. (2) Subtle sensor data tampering (Type 2) is the hardest to detect because the altered values remain within normal operating bounds. (3) Isolation Forest's advantage comes from modeling the joint distribution of features. (4) Neither method captures temporal patterns — a sequential detector (LSTM autoencoder or temporal Isolation Forest) would further improve detection of Type 2 and Type 3 attacks.

tasks.prompt: Implement a false data injection (FDI) attack simulation on a power system state estimator. Using pandapower, set up the IEEE 13-bus test feeder (or a simplified equivalent), run DC state estimation with simulated measurements, craft a stealth FDI attack vector that modifies 2 voltage-related measurements while maintaining consistency with the measurement Jacobian (so the attack bypasses Chi-squared bad data detection), and verify the attack succeeds by showing the corrupted state passes the bad data detection test. Report the attack vector, the corrupted state estimate, and the residual analysis.

network.description: Simplified IEEE 13-bus feeder modeled in pandapower. Use pandapower's built-in IEEE 13-bus case or construct a 6-bus simplified equivalent with realistic impedances.

state_estimation_setup.measurement_noise: Gaussian, sigma = 0.01 pu for voltage, sigma = 0.02 pu for power

bad_data_detection.method: Chi-squared test on weighted measurement residuals
bad_data_detection.formula: J(x) = sum((z_i - h_i(x_hat))^2 / sigma_i^2) compared to chi2_threshold(m-n, 0.95)

attack_specification.target_measurements: Modify exactly 2 voltage magnitude measurements at electrically adjacent buses

attack_construction.step_1: Extract the measurement Jacobian matrix H from the state estimator
attack_construction.step_2: Choose a target state perturbation vector c (nonzero at 1-2 state variables)
attack_construction.step_3: Compute the attack vector a = H * c
attack_construction.step_4: Add a to the original measurements to create corrupted measurements z_a = z + a
attack_construction.step_5: Run state estimation on z_a and verify J(x_a) < chi2_threshold

constraints.note: The corrupted state should differ from the true state by at most 0.03 pu on targeted buses, making the attack impactful but not so large as to be obviously physically impossible

success_criteria.must_demonstrate: The corrupted measurements pass the Chi-squared test (J(x_a) < threshold) while the estimated state is meaningfully different from the true state

instructions: Import pandapower and create the IEEE 13-bus network (use pandapower.networks.case_ieee_13 or build a simplified equivalent).
Run a power flow to obtain true bus voltages and power values.
Add Gaussian noise to create simulated measurements.
Set up the state estimator using pandapower.estimation module.
Run state estimation on the clean (noisy but unattacked) measurements, extract H matrix, compute J(x) and verify it passes Chi-squared test.
Construct the FDI attack: select a state perturbation vector c, compute a = H*c, add a to measurements.
Run state estimation on the corrupted measurements.
Verify: (a) J(x_a) < chi2_threshold (attack bypasses detection), (b) corrupted state differs from true state at targeted buses.
Produce a summary table showing true state, clean estimated state, and corrupted estimated state for all buses.
Plot the voltage profile across buses for true, clean estimate, and corrupted estimate.

tasks.ground_truth: The implementation should demonstrate: (1) A valid pandapower network with state estimation converging on clean measurements with J(x) well below the Chi-squared threshold (typically J(x) ~ 3-8 for the expected degrees of freedom vs threshold of ~15-20). (2) The H matrix should be extracted as an m x n matrix where m = number of measurements (typically 16-20) and n = number of state variables (12-13 for a 13-bus system). The matrix should have full column rank for observability. (3) The attack vector a = Hc should be computed for a chosen c that perturbs 1-2 bus voltage angles/magnitudes by 0.01-0.03 pu. Because a lies in the column space of H, the state estimator will find a new state x_a = x_true + c that perfectly explains the corrupted measurements, producing residuals identical to the pre-attack case. (4) The post-attack J(x_a) should be nearly identical to J(x) (within numerical precision), confirming the Chi-squared test is bypassed. (5) The voltage at targeted buses should show the intended perturbation (e.g., Bus 671 voltage estimated as 1.005 pu instead of true 0.990 pu). This demonstrates the fundamental vulnerability identified by Liu, Ning, and Reiter (2011): with knowledge of H, an attacker can construct undetectable measurement corruptions.
